narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive dedication in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60, which stands out above the typical student's 3.21. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated your risk of failure at 44.3%—high enough to lead to the regrettable rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your school decile rankings from Years 1 and 3, both at the 1st decile, far below the averages of around the 5th or 6th decile for most students, signaling to the model a notable dip in early academic performance compared to peers. Your LSAT score of 29.50, lower than the dataset average of 36.70, also played a key role in pushing the prediction toward higher failure risk, unlike your undergraduate GPA, which strongly pulled it in the favorable direction of passing. The next biggest factor after those deciles was your LSAT, reinforcing concerns about exam readiness relative to typical applicants. Smaller influences included your middle family income bracket, which slightly nudged toward failure risk compared to higher averages, while your full-time student status offered a minor positive boost, aligning with the vast majority of students. Overall, the model weighed your lower deciles and LSAT most heavily against your GPA strengths, painting a picture riskier than for students with more balanced top performances. We know this decision feels personal and tough, especially navigating these spaces as a Black woman, but it's purely data-driven to help the school select those with the strongest predicted bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the 36.70 average, strengthening your early-grade deciles through additional coursework or explanations in a reapplication, and highlighting any unique experiences that showcase your resilience. Many students in similar situations improve these metrics and succeed on reapplication—your above-average GPA is a great foundation to build on. Keep pushing; your determination will shine through.
